name: Relative Price of Finance

book_quotes.quote: Figure 10.7 tracks the relative price of finance (i/p), both variables now being indexed to 1947 as the base year. As a relative price of provision, this would be expected to essentially reflect the real cost of finance as in equation (10.8).

From 1857 to 1927 this ratio is relatively stable even through the Great Depression of 1873-1893, and its subsequent modest downward trend into the early 1920s is consistent with rapid innovations in finance.

The long bond yield was created by splicing together two long series: the railroad bond yield from 1857 to 1937 was annualized monthly data on American bond yields taken from Macaulay (Macaulay 1938, Appendix Table 10, A142-A161) ... The US Producer Price Index (Wholesale Price Index) for 1857-1976 is from (Jastram 1977, 145-146, table 7) and updated thereafter from the BLS using growth rates of the PPI.

formula: rpf_t = (i_t / i_1947) / (p_t / p_1947), where i = composite long bond yield (S1002-A) and p = composite PPI (S1002-B)

extension_candidates.subseries_label: Same FRED-based extensions as S1002 (AAA / PPIACO) then re-form ratio
concerns: No independent source; extension follows S1002 mechanics

methodology_notes: Pure transformation of S1002 sub-series; no new data ingest.
1947 chosen as base year (matches Shaikh's other current-cost benchmarks in Ch6/7).
Theoretical anchor: equation (10.8) ties i/p to real banking costs and the general rate of profit.
Per book p.466: ratio is relatively stable 1857-1927, then declines; structural break post-Greenspan attributed to active monetary policy keeping i down while p rises.
open_questions: Phase 4 to decide whether to re-index to a more recent base (e.g., 2010) for cross-comparison with modern series.

review_history.notes: CD2 S053 ported; quotes verified pp.466, 873
